published.content:
Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond ran a year-long field study of 5,179 customer support agents at a Fortune 500 software firm. Half worked with a generative AI assistant trained on the company's own chat history. Half worked without it. Across the full sample, agents with the assistant resolved 14% more issues per hour. The gain concentrated entirely at the bottom of the experience distribution. Novice agents in their first months posted a 34% productivity lift. Senior agents already at the top of the curve gained almost nothing.

The mechanism is the Week 6 lesson written in research form. The AI carried no innate edge over the senior staff. It encoded what those agents already knew. The training set was the firm's corpus of top-performing chat transcripts. The assistant pulled the phrases, escalation paths, and resolution moves the best agents had developed over years, and surfaced them in real time to the agent on the call. Senior staff gained little because the model carried no information they did not already have. Novices gained because they were now operating on a senior agent's playbook.

Week 6 asks the same question at a smaller scale. You spent four weeks getting reps on Claude. Some of those moves worked. The Week 6 challenge is to find one output you sent externally, then trace what Claude produced, what you changed, and why. That trace is the same move the Brynjolfsson firm made on its top performers. Once a move is traced, it can be reused. Once it is reused, it stops belonging to you. The capstone gets built on top of a stack of traces.

published.title: 57/43: How Claude Actually Gets Used at Work
published.content:
Anthropic's Economic Index, released February 10, 2025, analyzed millions of anonymized conversations on Claude.ai using Anthropic's Clio analysis system and the Department of Labor's O*NET task taxonomy. The report mapped what people actually use Claude for at work. The headline finding was the split between augmentation and automation. Fifty-seven percent of conversations involved Claude assisting a human through a task. Forty-three percent involved Claude completing a task on the user's behalf.

The breakdown matters more than the headline. Within augmentation, Anthropic identified three patterns: task iteration, where the user shapes Claude's output across multiple turns; learning, where the user works with Claude to understand a problem; and validation, where the user checks their own work against Claude's read. Within automation, Anthropic identified two patterns: directive use, where a single prompt produces a finished output, and feedback-loop use, where Claude completes a task with environmental feedback inside a tool.

Two implications for the pilot. First, augmentation through iteration is one of the dominant patterns Anthropic identified in real-world use. The pattern shows up across the kinds of work where users get useful output from Claude. Second, the Week 5 challenge in the pilot lives in exactly that pattern. Hand the build to a teammate, watch what breaks, fix it. The Economic Index gives the move an empirical home.

The Iterate and Inherit week asks DC CAP staff to do at the team level what the rest of the working economy is already doing at the individual level.
published.tryThis: Pull up your last AI conversation with Claude. Count the turns. One turn means you used directive mode, where a single prompt produced finished output. Multiple turns of refinement mean you used iteration mode. If your work this week was mostly directive, pick one task next week and force at least three iteration turns on it. Bring the before-and-after to AI Friday.

published.title: 1% of Companies Reach AI Maturity. The Wall Is Organizational Adoption.
published.content:
McKinsey's January 2025 Superagency in the Workplace report polled U.S. employees who use AI at work weekly and C-suite executives at the same companies. The finding that pulled focus: only 1% of leaders call their company's AI deployment 'mature,' McKinsey's label for the stage where AI is fully integrated into workflows and driving substantial business outcomes across the enterprise. The other 99% sit somewhere on the pilot-to-stall spectrum.

Access is broadly available. More than 9 in 10 companies plan to increase AI investment over the next three years. Half already report some operational gains. The wall sits at organizational adoption: leadership without a clear direction, governance lagging deployment, frontline workers using tools their leaders do not understand, and individual productivity gains failing to compound into team-level capability.

Week 5 of the AI governance pilot is the move built against that wall. The Week 4 build sat with one author who knew where it worked and where it broke. Week 5 hands the build to a teammate and watches what happens. Iteration is the test. The artifact that survives a teammate, a fix, and a documented checkpoint becomes the team-level asset McKinsey describes only 1% of companies as producing.

The trend implication for DC CAP: every individual using Claude well builds toward AI maturity. Maturity arrives the moment a build leaves its author, gets fixed by someone else, and produces work the team relies on. The pilot's design assumes that handoff is the harder problem.
published.tryThis: Pick the build you handed to a teammate this week. Sit with the teammate for ten minutes. Note one place the build worked, one place it broke, and one fix you both agree on. That note is the artifact McKinsey calls AI maturity. The build is the seed. The handoff is what makes it real for the team.

published.title: Inheritance Changes the Tier on Every File You Bundled
published.content:
Week 5 of the AI governance pilot is iterate and scope. The Week 4 build goes to a teammate, breaks somewhere, and gets fixed. The pilot hub flags a governance question most builders miss in that handoff: iteration changes who can use what. If your v2 expands the audience, the data tier of every reference file inside the build expands with it.

The framework is unambiguous on what each tier permits. Tier 4 public materials carry no restriction. Tier 3 internal documents like strategic plans, program models, and de-identified aggregate outcomes sit under open access for DC CAP licensed users. Tier 2 sensitive content covers budget models, MOU terms, and small-cell aggregates with N below 10, all requiring role-specific access and de-identification verification before upload. Tier 1 PII and FERPA-protected records stay prohibited from Claude in any form.

A Project that worked for one author with a tight reference pack assumes the author's trust permissions. The moment a teammate inherits it, those permissions stop being implicit. A budget model loaded for a Director's planning use becomes a Tier 2 file in a coaching team's hands. A partner MOU referenced for a single email becomes a re-disclosure when the Project ships to four colleagues.

The discipline is mechanical. Before broadening access, list every file in the Project's reference panel, tag each one to a tier, and decide whether the new audience is authorized for the highest tier on the list. If anything is Tier 2, role-restrict access. If anything is Tier 1, remove it and rebuild the reference around aggregated or public substitutes.
published.tryThis: Open the Project you'll hand to a teammate this week. List every file in the reference panel. Tag each one Tier 1, 2, 3, or 4 against Section 6 of the framework. If the highest-tier file in the pack outranks the audience you're expanding to, role-restrict access or rebuild the reference around aggregated or public substitutes before you ship the v2.

published.content:
Week 4 asks every participant to build something worth keeping. The challenge requires loading at least one outside source (a research doc, a peer playbook, a verified data table, the brand guide, an Anthropic skill) and writing it into the procedure. The trap is pasting the source into a reference file and assuming Claude will read it. Outputs sound the same as before. The build does not earn its keep.

Extraction is the move. A source changes output when you pull the specific rules, numbers, and anti-patterns out of it and write them into the custom-instructions block or SKILL.md procedure. Three lines of doing.

Step one: open Claude with the source loaded. Ask: "Read this document. Extract every concrete instruction, threshold, or anti-pattern that should change how a draft on [your task] gets written. List them as bullets, with the page or section number for each." This produces a list you can audit. Numbers, named patterns, prohibitions: the parts of the source that have an opinion.

Step two: filter. Strike anything generic. Keep what is specific to your task and verifiable against the source.

Step three: write the kept items into the procedure. In a Project, add them under the custom-instructions block as standards the draft must meet. In a Skill, write them into the SKILL.md as named rules. Cite the source so the next reader can trace any rule back to where it came from.

The build now carries the source's authority. A teammate inheriting it gets the rules and the citation in one move. That is what Week 4 means by a build that earns its keep.

published.title: Extract, Encode, Cite: How to Load an Outside Source Into a Build
published.tryThis: Pick the outside source you intend to load this week. Open Claude with it attached. Run the extraction prompt. Print the bullet list. Strike anything generic. Move the keepers into your custom-instructions block or SKILL.md, cite the section number for each, then run a draft and check whether the rules actually shaped the output.

published.content:
A 2023 field experiment from Harvard Business School, MIT, Wharton, and BCG ran 758 consultants through 18 realistic business tasks. Half had access to GPT-4. Inside what the researchers called the AI frontier (the tasks the model could handle), consultants with GPT-4 finished 12% more tasks, 25% faster, and produced work judged 40% higher in quality. Outside the frontier, where the model gave plausible but wrong answers, AI users did worse than the control group.

The finding worth carrying into Week 5: the productivity lift came from how people used the model. Pure access captured only a fraction of the available gain. The rest came from iteration. The researchers labeled two working patterns. Centaurs split work cleanly, handing whole pieces to the model. Cyborgs wove iteration through every step: draft, critique, redraft, hand to a colleague, fix what broke. Both patterns produced gains. Both required the user to know where the frontier sat and stop trusting the model past the edge.

That is the Week 5 inheritance test in research form. Week 4 asked you to build something worth keeping. Week 5 asks you to hand the build to a teammate and watch where it breaks. The Dell'Acqua paper says the same thing the pilot says. A build earns its keep through iteration. The first version is a guess about where the frontier sits. The second version, written after a teammate hits the edge, is the one that knows.

The trap is treating the first output as the final answer. The Cyborgs in the study did not.

published.title: 17% to 33%: When "Hallucination-Free" Tools Still Hallucinate
published.content:
Stanford's RegLab and Human-Centered AI Institute tested two AI legal research products that marketed themselves as hallucination-free. The 2024 study evaluated Lexis+ AI and Thomson Reuters' AI legal research tools against a benchmark of legal queries with known correct answers. The tools hallucinated between 17% and 33% of the time. Both products had been pitched to lawyers as solving the hallucination problem.

The researchers defined a hallucination as any answer that was either factually wrong or that cited a source the tool got wrong. The errors were not edge cases. Tools fabricated case names, misquoted holdings, and cited statutes that did not say what the tool claimed. Two of the country's largest legal-tech vendors shipped products with that error rate, alongside marketing claims that did not hold up.

The Stanford finding is the closest public analog to what the Discernment competency asks each pilot participant to develop. Vendor claims about reliability do not transfer to your specific task without verification. A tool can be useful 80% of the time and still produce errors at a rate that, if untouched, embarrasses your team and your scholars. The 17% to 33% range is the empirical case for keeping a human verifier in the loop on work that reaches a scholar, a partner, a funder, or the board.

The pilot's working assumption is that AI changes the speed of drafting, the breadth of input synthesis, and the floor of staff capacity. The standard your work has to meet stays the same. Stanford's 1-in-6 rate is what that standard costs to ignore.

published.content:
Anthropic released its March 2026 Economic Index report this spring, the third large-scale dataset on how Claude actually gets used. One finding sits directly in DC CAP's lane. Educational instruction tasks (coursework support, tutoring, instructional material development) rose from 9% to 13% of all Claude conversations. That is the fastest-growing task category in the Index. On Claude.ai specifically, educational instruction now accounts for 16% of conversations, four times the rate seen on the API. Education has moved into Claude's mainstream.

The augmentation share moved with it. 57% of conversations were augmentation in the current Index, where the user iterates with Claude through multiple turns of refinement. 43% were automation, where the user delegates a task and accepts the output. This is up from a 52/45 split in the prior Index, a meaningful shift in the direction of collaboration.

Two implications for the pilot. First, the kind of work DC CAP staff do (coaching, communication, instruction, family-facing explanation) is the work Claude is being adopted for fastest in the wild. The pilot is running with that trend. Second, the augmentation share rising is consistent with what the pilot has been teaching. Multi-turn collaboration produces better output on tasks where the standard depends on judgment.

The Economic Index gives the pilot a market check. The same job-shaped tasks DC CAP is bringing into Claude (drafting, explanation, structured feedback) are the ones the broader user base is converging on. The strategic question for Week 4 is what your team's split looks like, and whether it should.

published.content:
Section 7.3 of DC CAP's AI Governance Framework names the line every pilot member is accountable to: "All external communications remain staff-authored; Claude is a tool in the drafting process." Section 7.2 backs it: "Staff remain accountable for the quality and accuracy of all work product." Those two sentences carry the pilot's deepest expectation. The author is the person whose name appears on the email, the proposal, the report. Claude drafts. The author signs.

The expectation becomes operational this week. In Week 4 you trade work with a colleague. They will read what you sent and tell you what they noticed. The feedback runs deeper when the draft already passed through your hands. A forwarded first output makes your colleague your QA layer. The peer review collapses into proofreading.

The verification pass has three checks. Check the facts first. Every claim about scholar outcomes, partner counts, deadlines, or policy must trace to a source you can cite. Check the voice second. Claude can match a tone and also smooth your edges into someone else's. Check the standard third. Read each sentence as if you were defending it in a board meeting or in a parent conversation. That is the bar the framework sets when it puts staff signatures on work product.

The framework treats Claude as a drafting accelerant. The deliverable carries your signature. Walk every output through the verification pass before it leaves your screen. That makes the peer feedback you get this week an audit of your judgment.

published.content:
A Claude Project's custom-instructions box is three short fields most people leave near-empty. Filling them well is the move that separates a Project from a folder of one-off chats. The pilot hub's worked example, for a Partner University Updates Project, sets the pattern.

"You help draft communications to our 13 university partner admissions contacts. Tone is collegial and supportive — these are year-round relationships. Always include specific dates and next steps. Never reference individual student names or financial details."

Three sentences, three loads. The first names the audience and the relationship: who Claude is talking to and on what footing. The second encodes what good output looks like: dates, next steps, the working standards your team already uses. The third sets the governance line: no student names, no financial details, the Tier 3 data tier baked into the workspace.

The same shape scales. A coach's "Cohort Check-In" Project might read: "You help me draft outreach to first-generation scholars at [partner]. Tone is direct and warm. Always offer one concrete next step. Never reference grades or family details." A development "Funder Update" Project: "You help draft donor stewardship to lead-tier funders. Tone matches our last impact report. Always tie a scholar outcome to the funder's investment area. Never share aggregate metrics that haven't appeared in a published source."

Three sentences, every Project. The hour spent writing them is the hour your team stops re-explaining context the next time the work shows up.

published.content:
A 2023 study from Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond at MIT and Stanford tracked 5,179 customer support agents over a year as their company rolled out a generative-AI assistant. The result: 14% average productivity lift, with novice agents gaining 34% while top performers saw little change. The paper sits in the NBER working-paper series.

The mechanism is the part worth studying. The AI tool encoded the patterns top agents already used: how to phrase a hard message, when to escalate, what to ask before answering. New hires got those patterns on day one. Productivity gains held over the full year. The senior agents had nothing new to learn because the tool was running their playbook back at them.

That is the Description lesson Week 3 of the pilot is asking you to take seriously. Whatever you describe well becomes the part that scales. Whatever stays in your head stays a one-time effort. The four containers in this week's reading (a one-shot prompt, a Claude Project, a Skill, an automation) exist because each one reuses a different piece of your description. A Project reuses context. A Skill reuses a move. An automation reuses a trigger.

Naming the container is half the work. The other half is writing the description so the move travels: across any partner, any topic, next week's version of yourself. Description done well is how your H1 reps become H2 inheritance, a move the team can run without rebuilding the prompt every Tuesday.

published.title: 75% and 78%: Why the Pilot Exists
published.content:
Three out of four knowledge workers now use AI at work. Seventy-eight percent of them bring their own tools to the job, without telling their employer. Those are two anchor findings in the Microsoft and LinkedIn 2024 Work Trend Index, which surveyed 31,000 workers across 31 countries.

The 75% says the adoption question is settled at the population level. The workforce did not wait for a policy. People found tools that make their work faster and started using them. DC CAP's decision to build a structured governance framework answers a real behavioral trend, which means your participation anchors that trend inside an organization that still protects scholars, partners, and organizational trust.

The 78% says where the risk lives. When three in four staff will use AI regardless, and four in five of those will bring personal tools if the organization does not sanction any, the governance question shifts. Every AI interaction sits somewhere. Either inside a data classification and consent model you can see, or inside a personal ChatGPT tab nobody has audited. "Shadow AI" is the industry term for the second category, and it is the predictable outcome of a governance vacuum.

The pilot's design responds to this research directly. The 4-tier data classification, the governance pulse questions, and the sanctioned Claude workspace give staff a clean path. When a colleague asks why DC CAP is running this pilot now, the Microsoft numbers are the answer. The adoption is already happening. The pilot gives it structure.

published.title: 25% Faster or 19% More Errors: What Delegation Actually Costs
published.content: In a 2023 field study, Harvard and BCG researchers gave GPT-4 to 758 BCG consultants working on 18 realistic business tasks. Two results anchor Week 2 of this pilot. On tasks inside AI's capability frontier, consultants using AI finished 25% faster and produced output rated 40% higher in quality. On tasks outside AI's frontier, the same consultants using AI were 19 percentage points more likely to produce incorrect answers than the control group that did not use AI. The pattern applies directly to DC CAP work. Claude accelerates tasks where the pattern is clear and the standard is well-defined: formatting enrollment data into a briefing, drafting a partner reminder email from a template, restructuring meeting notes into action items. Claude degrades output on tasks where the judgment belongs to you: whether a scholar's documented circumstances warrant a policy exception, whether a partnership shift is worth its political cost, how a contested decision should land with a specific donor. Every team in this pilot sits where those 758 consultants did. You have a tool that accelerates work and can also quietly degrade it. The variable is Delegation judgment. Naming whether a task sits inside or outside Claude's reliable frontier before you open a session is the move that separates the 25% speed gain from the 19% error rate. Week 2 makes this the central question. The BCG study gives the pilot a quantitative anchor. Good Delegation compounds. Bad Delegation produces polished errors.

published.title: Automation or Augmentation: Name the Mode Before You Prompt
published.content: Every time you open Claude, you make a choice about mode. You are either asking Claude to do the task (automation) or asking it to help you think through it (augmentation). The shape of a good prompt changes based on the answer. Week 2 of the pilot frames this as the difference between Automation and Augmentation, two of the three interaction modalities in DC CAP's AI Fluency Framework. Each mode is valid. Each requires different prompt habits. Automation works when the task is well-defined and the output pattern is clear. Drafting a templated partner reminder email, formatting meeting notes into a summary, or converting a CSV into a budget table are automation prompts. You specify what you want, Claude produces it, you review and send. Augmentation works when the task requires judgment and you benefit from a thinking partner. Structuring an argument for a board memo, stress-testing a strategic decision, or diagnosing why a student retention pattern looks strange are augmentation prompts. You describe the problem, Claude offers frames and options, and you decide what holds. The one-minute test before each Claude session: name what kind of help you need before you type. If you want output, lead with the output specification. If you want thinking, lead with the problem and invite frames before answers. Confusion between these two modes is a common source of friction in early Claude use. A prompt written as automation when you needed augmentation produces a polished document you then have to rebuild anyway. Name the mode first, then write the prompt.

published.title: Diligence: Your Name Is Still on the Work
published.content: When you send a scholarship renewal reminder that Claude helped you draft, your name goes on that email. When a board summary includes enrollment figures Claude formatted, your credibility backs those numbers. Diligence, the fourth competency in DC CAP's AI Fluency Framework, is the practice of taking full responsibility for outputs created with AI assistance. The framework breaks Diligence into three layers. Creation Diligence means using AI tools in line with ethical and legal best practices, including identifying potential biases in outputs. Transparency Diligence means being clear about AI involvement when distributing work products and understanding what your audience, industry, and legal context expect. Deployment Diligence means verifying every output before it leaves your hands, checking the facts that matter, and owning the risks. On March 24, 2026, the U.S. Department of Labor launched "Make America AI-Ready," a free AI literacy course built around five core areas. The fifth area, "Using AI Responsibly," maps directly to what DC CAP calls Diligence. The federal government is formalizing the expectation that workers who use AI take accountability for what AI helps them produce. The practical test: if a colleague asked "Did you check this?" and you could not explain exactly what you verified and how, Diligence has not been practiced. The standard is simple. Every output you share carries your professional judgment, regardless of what Claude produced.
published.tryThis: Pull up the last document Claude helped you create. Read it as though you were handing it to your supervisor with your name on it. Identify one claim or number you did not independently verify, and verify it now.

published.title: The Before-and-After Prompt
published.content: Most people write their first prompt like a search query: short, vague, hoping Claude reads their mind. Here's the single most impactful change you can make: add context before your request. Instead of 'Write an email about the deadline,' try: 'I'm a program manager at DC CAP writing to scholarship applicants who haven't submitted their FAFSA yet. Write a warm, encouraging reminder email that explains why the FAFSA matters for their DC CAP scholarship and gives them a clear deadline. Keep it under 200 words.' The difference is dramatic. Context transforms Claude from a generic text generator into a knowledgeable colleague.
published.tryThis: Take a task you did last week. Write the worst possible prompt for it (under 10 words). Then rewrite it with context, audience, format, and constraints. Compare the outputs.

published.title: Delegation: The Decision Before the Prompt
published.content: Delegation is the competency most people skip. Before you type a single word into Claude, you've already made a delegation decision: 'Is this a task AI should help with?' Good delegation means understanding both what Claude does well (drafting, summarizing, analyzing patterns, formatting) and what requires human judgment (final decisions on student welfare, sensitive communications, anything involving live relationships). The Delegation Map activity in Week 3 will help you map your recurring tasks on a grid. For now, start noticing: every time you open Claude, you've made a delegation choice. Was it a good one?
published.tryThis: Keep a running list today of every task you considered using Claude for. At the end of the day, mark each one: 'Good delegation' or 'Should have done this myself.' You'll start seeing patterns.

published.title: Chat vs. Cowork: Pick the Right Tool
published.content: Chat (claude.ai) is your everyday thinking partner. Use it for quick drafts, brainstorming, one-off analysis, and anything where you need text output you'll copy elsewhere. Cowork (the desktop app) is your production studio. Use it when you need actual files — Word docs, PowerPoints, Excel workbooks — or when you want DC CAP's built-in Skills to handle the formatting and voice rules for you. A good rule of thumb: if you're going to copy-paste Claude's output into another application, Chat is fine. If you want Claude to produce the final deliverable directly, use Cowork.
published.tryThis: Open both Chat and Cowork today. Ask each the same question: 'Draft a one-paragraph summary of what DC CAP does for a new donor.' Compare the experience. Notice which felt more natural for this task.

published.title: The 92/7 Gap: What Nonprofit AI Adoption Data Reveals About Your Pilot
published.content: The most striking finding in the 2026 Nonprofit AI Adoption Report from Virtuous and FundraisingAI is a ratio that should reframe how you approach this pilot. Ninety-two percent of nonprofits now use AI in some capacity. Seven percent report major improvements in organizational capability. The adoption plateau is real, and most of the sector is sitting on it. The gap is not evidence that AI does not work. It reflects a specific structural failure: organizations that plateau treat AI as a personal productivity tool, used reactively and in isolation. Organizations that reach the 7% have embedded AI into team-level workflows with documented practices and clear governance. They have answered two questions that most have not: which tasks belong to AI, and how do you verify what it produces? The Omatic Fourth Annual Nonprofit Technology Ecosystem Trends Report, released April 8, 2026, adds a connected finding: nonprofit teams are drowning in data and struggling to trust it. AI increasingly serves as the interpretive layer between raw data and strategic decisions — but only for organizations with governance structures that define what data can enter AI systems and what cannot. DC CAP's four-tier data classification is exactly that kind of infrastructure. The DC CAP pilot's three-tier KPI framework reflects a deliberate response to the sector gap. Engagement is where 92% of nonprofits already live. The pilot targets Tier 2 proficiency and Tier 3 documented impact. The workflow redesign requirement in Week 3 is the structural move that separates repeatable practice from one-off experimentation. When you complete that template, you are building what fewer than one in ten nonprofits has managed: an evidence base for AI that actually shifts outcomes.
published.tryThis: Open the KPI Framework page on the pilot hub and review the Tier 3 impact metrics. Identify one recurring task in your role — something you do at least weekly — that you have already tried with Claude. Sketch the old process and the new process in two or three sentences each. That sketch is the starting point for your formal workflow redesign in Week 3.

published.title: Discernment Starts With One Question
published.content: Claude will almost always give you a confident, well-formatted answer. That confidence is the exact moment your Discernment competency matters most. Discernment, one of the four core competencies in DC CAP's AI Fluency Framework (alongside Delegation, Description, and Diligence), is the ability to critically evaluate AI-generated outputs for quality, accuracy, relevance, and bias. In practice, it begins with a single question: "How would I verify this if a new colleague handed it to me?" Anthropic's own research found that structured prompting techniques like step-by-step reasoning and few-shot examples improved accuracy by 20% in one enterprise case study. That means a well-structured prompt yields better output. But even a well-structured prompt can produce plausible-sounding errors. A scholarship renewal reminder that cites the wrong GPA threshold. A board summary that rounds enrollment numbers in a misleading direction. A donor email that attributes a statistic to a report that doesn't exist. These errors look polished. They read smoothly. And that is precisely why Discernment requires deliberate practice. The DC CAP AI Fluency Framework breaks Discernment into three layers: evaluating output quality, assessing whether the human-AI collaboration is productive, and gathering feedback to refine AI behaviors over time. You are building a verification habit. The federal government has begun formalizing AI literacy as a workforce competency, with the Department of Labor publishing an AI literacy framework in early 2026. DC CAP's 4D framework already embeds this skill. Your job now is to practice it consistently.
published.tryThis: Open your most recent Claude conversation. Pick one factual claim in the output and verify it using an independent source. Notice how long the verification takes versus how long you spent reviewing the original output. That ratio tells you something about your current Discernment habits.

published.title: The Five-Second Question That Protects Student Data
published.content: Before you paste anything into Claude, ask one question: what tier is this data? That five-second pause is the most important governance habit you will build during this pilot. DC CAP classifies all organizational data into four tiers. Tier 4 is public: annual reports, website content, published research, IPEDS data. Tier 3 is internal: program models, strategic documents, aggregated and de-identified outcomes like overall retention rates. Tier 2 is sensitive: internal financial records, staff performance data, and small-cell aggregates where fewer than ten individuals could be re-identified. Tier 1 is restricted: individual student records, Social Security numbers, any data protected under FERPA. During the pilot, you are approved to use Tier 3 and Tier 4 data with Claude. Tier 2 requires specific authorization and de-identification verification. Tier 1 data never enters Claude under any circumstances. The governance framework sets a clear target: zero Tier 1 or Tier 2 data incidents during the pilot. This structure matters beyond DC CAP's walls. The 2026 Nonprofit AI Adoption Report from Virtuous and Fundraising.AI found that 47% of nonprofits using AI have no governance policy at all, despite 92% adoption. Organizations without governance frameworks tend to skip outcome measurement, avoid shared workflows, and plateau at marginal efficiency gains. DC CAP's four-tier classification gives every staff member a concrete decision rule for every interaction with Claude. The habit is simple: before you upload, paste, or reference any information, name the tier. When the classification feels ambiguous, treat the data as the higher (more restricted) tier. That default protects students, partners, and the organization.
published.tryThis: Open the Data Classification Practice on the pilot hub site and complete all 10 scenarios. Each one presents a real DC CAP data example and asks you to assign the correct tier. Notice which scenarios trip you up. Those are the edge cases worth discussing with your team.

published.title: Anthropic Built an AI That Finds Security Flaws and Chose Governance First
published.content: This week, Anthropic announced Project Glasswing, a cybersecurity initiative built around a new frontier model called Claude Mythos Preview. In early testing, the model identified thousands of previously unknown security flaws in widely used software, some of which had gone undetected for decades despite extensive human review. Anthropic made a deliberate choice: the model is too powerful to release publicly. Instead, the company committed $100 million in usage credits and assembled a coalition of major technology and financial organizations, including AWS, Google, Microsoft, CrowdStrike, and the Linux Foundation, to use the model strictly for defensive security work. The response was immediate. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell convened bank CEOs to discuss the implications for financial system security. Why does this story matter for you? Project Glasswing is a governance decision at planetary scale. Anthropic had a capability that could cause serious harm if deployed without boundaries, so the organization built a framework first: restricted access, vetted partners, specific use cases, clear accountability. That logic mirrors what you are learning in this pilot. DC CAP's four-tier data classification system operates on the same principle. You assess the sensitivity of information before it enters Claude. You match capability to appropriate use. You default to the more restrictive tier when the classification is ambiguous. The organizations benefiting from frontier AI capabilities right now are the ones that invested in governance infrastructure before the capabilities arrived. That is exactly what you are building during this pilot.
published.tryThis: Read the one-page Project Glasswing announcement at anthropic.com/glasswing. Identify one governance decision Anthropic made, such as restricted access or vetted-partner-only deployment. Then connect it to a parallel decision in DC CAP's own governance framework. Write the connection in two sentences. That exercise is Discernment applied to real-world AI policy.

published.title: Your Cowork Workspace Just Became Enterprise Infrastructure
published.content: This week, Anthropic moved Claude Cowork from research preview to general availability. The desktop application you have been using during this pilot is now an enterprise-grade product available on both macOS and Windows for all paid subscribers. For organizations on enterprise plans, the update introduces administrative controls that let IT teams define which capabilities different staff groups can access, set spending limits by team, and track usage patterns across the organization. Anthropic also launched Claude Managed Agents in public beta, a hosted service where organizations can deploy AI agents that handle multi-step tasks autonomously. Three organizations confirmed their early adoption at launch: Notion for collaborative workspace tasks, Rakuten for enterprise communication agents, and Sentry for automated debugging. These developments connect directly to what you are building toward in the pilot. The capstone showcase asks each leader to define what goes into their unit's Claude workspace: which organizational knowledge to load, which skills and projects to implement, and which practices to streamline. The enterprise features announced this week are the production infrastructure those capstone designs will eventually run on. Consider what this means for your role. The Cowork workspace you build during the pilot is a prototype for how your entire unit will interact with Claude after the pilot ends. The skills, projects, and reference materials you curate become shared infrastructure your team inherits. The administrative controls Anthropic just shipped give organizations like DC CAP the ability to manage those workspaces responsibly at scale, with clear boundaries around who can access what. AI is moving from individual productivity tool to organizational infrastructure. Your capstone work is preparing DC CAP to make that transition deliberately.
published.tryThis: Open your Cowork desktop app and look at your current Projects. Ask yourself: if my entire unit needed to use this workspace starting next month, what two reference files or standing instructions would I add first? Write them down. You have just started your capstone planning.

published.title: Give Claude a Project, Not Just a Prompt
published.content: Every time you open a new conversation outside a Project, you spend time re-establishing context. You re-explain your role, your audience, the tone you want. Projects in Cowork eliminate that repetition entirely. A Project is a persistent workspace where you bundle instructions, reference files, and links that Claude loads automatically every time you start a new task. Think of it as a briefing packet Claude reads before you say a word. For scholarship communications, you could create a Project that includes DC CAP's voice guidelines, the current application timeline, and a standing instruction like "Always reference DCTAG documentation requirements when writing to applicants." For board reporting, a separate Project might hold last quarter's metrics, your executive summary template, and formatting rules. Claude's memory is scoped to each Project, meaning context it learns from your scholarship work stays in the scholarship workspace and does not bleed into your donor communications. This matters when you manage several distinct workstreams. The practical payoff is speed. Anthropic's support documentation describes Projects as dedicated workspaces where context, instructions, files, memory, and scheduled tasks all live together in one place. When you reduce the setup time on each conversation, the gains compound quickly. You set up the workspace once, and every future task in that Project inherits the full context automatically. Your third task in a Project benefits from the context Claude retained from the first two.
published.tryThis: Create your first Cowork Project today. Pick one recurring task area, such as student emails or weekly reports. Add a two-sentence instruction describing the audience and tone, plus one reference file Claude should always consult. Run one task and notice how much less setup the conversation requires.
